narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have them paused like this feels deeply unfair, and I'm here to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped, landing at a 48% chance of not passing. Picture it like a story of your journey so far: your Year 3 grades placing you in the bottom decile of your school stood out as the biggest shadow, whispering doubts about sustaining top performance under law's intense pressures, even more heavily than your Year 1 decile of 2, which also pulled the narrative toward struggle. Your LSAT score of 33 added to that cautious tone, signaling the model that mastering the bar's rigorous reasoning might demand extra hurdles for you. Layered on top was your middle family income bracket, which the model linked to fewer safety nets during those grueling study marathons, and your background as a Black male, where patterns from past students showed steeper climbs to bar passage. Your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.7 offered a hopeful counterpoint, nudging things slightly toward success, as did your gender in a positive light, but choosing part-time status tipped the balance back, hinting at divided focus between work and the bar's demands. Together, these threads wove a picture of resilience tested by early academic dips and real-world pulls, making the model hesitate on your application's promise. This isn't the end of your story—many have rewritten similar chapters by targeting those Year 1 and 3 deciles with focused prep or full-time commitment. You've got a solid UGPA foundation; lean into it, and doors will open wider. Keep pushing—you're closer than it feels.
